intended_use: The AHI System is intended for use by healthcare professionals managing patients 18 years or older who are receiving continuous physiological monitoring with electrocardiography (ECG) in hospitals. AH
intended_use_full: The AHI System is intended for use by healthcare professionals managing patients 18 years or older who are receiving continuous physiological monitoring with electrocardiography (ECG) in hospitals. AHI provides a frequently updated binary output over time based on pattern analysis of a lead-II ECG waveform intended to describe a patient's hemodynamic status and indicate if a patient is showing signs of hemodynamic stability or instability. AHI-PI provides the clinician with physiological insight into a patient's likelihood of a future episode of hemodynamic instability. The goal of this adjunctive monitoring method is to enable identification of patients who are showing hemodynamic instability or are likely to experience a future episode of hemodynamic instability, and to allow clinicians an opportunity to increase vigilance. This device is intended for adjunctive use with other physical vital sign parameters and patient information and is not intended to independently direct therapy.
target_condition: Hemodynamic instability, defined as hypotension (systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg or mean arterial pressure <70 mmHg) combined with tachycardia (heart rate ≥100 bpm)
ground_truth: Continuous vital signs reference standard: hypotension (systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg or mean arterial pressure <70 mmHg) combined with tachycardia (heart rate ≥100 bpm) measured continuously via arterial line monitoring
other_metrics: For AHI-PI: Green (Low Risk) indicator—0.7% probability of hemodynamic instability episode in next 1 hour [95% CI: 0.4%-1.3%]; Yellow (Moderate Risk) indicator—6.5% probability [95% CI: 3.7%-10.3%], 9x more likely than Green; Red (High Risk) indicator—35.9% probability [95% CI: 28.1%-44.0%], 51x more likely than Green. Lead time: 89% of high-risk patients predicted deterioration with median lead time of 48 minutes. Overall prevalence of hemodynamic instability in study population: 10.5%.
